<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each)

### **Q1. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set A]**
**Define 3D printing (additive manufacturing) in the context of biomedical engineering. List any four biomedical applications.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

> **Definition (1.5 marks):** 3D printing, also called additive manufacturing, is a process of fabricating three-dimensional solid objects from a digital CAD model by the sequential addition of material layer by layer, with biomedical applications involving biomaterials such as polymers, metals, ceramics, and living cells.

**Four biomedical applications (1.5 marks):**
1. Patient-specific orthopedic and dental implants.
2. Tissue-engineered scaffolds for bone and cartilage regeneration.
3. Anatomical models for pre-surgical planning and rehearsal.
4. Bioprinted tissues and organ constructs for regenerative medicine.

---

### **Q2. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper]**
**List the major 3D printing technologies used in fabricating biomedical implants and scaffolds. State one biomedical use of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Technology | Biomedical Use |
|---|---|
| **SLA** | Surgical guides and dental aligners |
| **FDM** | Bone scaffolds from PCL/PLA |
| **SLS** | Porous orthopedic implants |
| **SLM/DMLS** | Custom Ti hip/knee implants |
| **Bioprinting (Extrusion)** | Skin patches, cartilage tissue |

> **Examiner's Allocation:** Technology names = 1.5 marks; Uses = 1.5 marks.

---

## 5.2 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) — *Internal Choice Pattern*

> **KTU 2024 ESE Convention:** Each Part B question carries 14 marks split into sub-parts (a) 7 marks + (b) 7 marks.

---

### **Question A (14 Marks) — *Set 1: Tissue Engineering & Scaffolds***

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** Explain the role of 3D printing in tissue engineering. Discuss the material, design, and biological requirements of an ideal bone scaffold. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Role of 3D printing in tissue engineering (3 marks):** Tissue engineering requires a 3D porous scaffold that supports cell attachment, proliferation, and differentiation, ultimately guiding the regeneration of functional tissue. 3D printing (especially FDM, SLA, and extrusion bioprinting) enables the fabrication of scaffolds with **patient-specific geometry**, **controlled internal porosity (50–80 %)**, and **interconnected pore networks** of size **200–500 μm** that are otherwise impossible with conventional manufacturing.

> **Material requirements (2 marks):** The scaffold material must be **biocompatible** (ISO 10993), **biodegradable** at a rate matching tissue regeneration, mechanically strong enough to bear physiological loads, and processable without releasing toxic by-products. Common materials include PCL, PLA, PLGA, hydroxyapatite (HA), β-tricalcium phosphate (β-TCP), and bio-composites.

> **Design and biological requirements (2 marks):**
> - **Porosity:** 50–80 % for cell infiltration and vascularization.
> - **Pore size:** 200–500 μm for osteogenesis.
> - **Interconnectivity:** Fully open pores for nutrient/waste transport.
> - **Surface chemistry:** Roughness and hydrophilicity to promote cell adhesion.
> - **Mechanical strength:** Match trabecular bone (compressive strength 2–12 MPa).

---

**(b)** With a neat flowchart, describe the complete workflow of 3D printing a patient-specific bone scaffold from CT scan to implantation. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer (Step-by-step):**

1. **CT/MRI scanning of the bone defect site** — yields DICOM voxel data (1 mark).
2. **Image segmentation** using Mimics, 3D Slicer, or InVesalius — converts voxels to 3D triangulated mesh (1 mark).
3. **STL export and CAD design** — generation of patient-specific geometry; CAD editing to add internal porous lattice using tools like nTopology (1 mark).
4. **Slicing and toolpath generation** — using Cura or Simplify3D; G-code generation with layer thickness ~ 100–200 μm (1 mark).
5. **3D printing** — material selection (PCL, PLA, HA composite) and FDM/robocasting execution (1 mark).
6. **Post-processing** — support removal, surface treatment, ethanol washing, drying (1 mark).
7. **Sterilization and quality control** — EtO or gamma sterilization; mechanical and biocompatibility testing; surgical implantation (1 mark).

> Refer **Figure 4.1** (Section 4) of these notes for the complete functional block flow.

---

### **Question B (14 Marks) — *Set 2: Bioprinting & Patient-Specific Implants***

**[KTU University Exam – Model Question Paper, KTU 2024 Scheme]**

**(a)** What is bioprinting? Compare the three major bioprinting approaches (inkjet, extrusion, and laser-assisted) in terms of resolution, cell viability, and applicable tissues. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

> **Definition of bioprinting (1.5 marks):** Bioprinting is the additive manufacturing of living tissue constructs by the precise, computer-controlled spatial deposition of **bioinks** (composed of living cells embedded in hydrogel matrices) to fabricate 3D tissue-like or organ-like structures for regenerative medicine and drug testing.

> **Comparison Table (5.5 marks):**

| Parameter | Inkjet Bioprinting | Extrusion Bioprinting | Laser-Assisted Bioprinting |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Resolution** | 20–100 μm (high) | 200–500 μm (medium) | > 5 μm (very high) |
| **Cell viability** | 70–95 % | 40–80 % (shear stress) | > 95 % (no nozzle) |
| **Throughput** | High | High (large constructs) | Low (slow) |
| **Applicable tissues** | Skin, retina, liver patches | Skin, cartilage, bone, large constructs | High-precision cell patterns, skin |
| **Cell density** | Low–Medium | High (cell pellets) | Low–Medium |
| **Viscosity range** | Low viscosity bioinks (3–12 mPa·s) | Wide range (30 mPa·s to > 600 mPa·s) | Low viscosity preferred |

---

**(b)** Explain any four clinical applications of 3D printing in orthopedics, dentistry, and surgical planning. **(7 marks)**

**Model Answer:**

1. **Customized Orthopedic Implants (2 marks):** 3D-printed Ti-6Al-4V acetabular cups, tibial trays, and spinal cages are designed from patient CT data, achieving superior fit and bone ingrowth via porous surfaces. Example: Conformis knee implants and Stryker Tritanium cups.

2. **Patient-Specific Cranial/Maxillofacial Implants (2 marks):** PEEK and titanium cranial plates printed from defect geometry restore skull contour after trauma or tumor resection. Bioactive coatings (HA) accelerate osseointegration.

3. **Dental Applications (1.5 marks):** Surgical drill guides, aligners (SLA-printed polymer), and custom abutments/crowns (Co-Cr SLM) are printed with micrometer accuracy, reducing chair-time and improving outcomes.

4. **Surgical Planning Models (1.5 marks):** Patient-specific anatomical replicas printed in polymer allow surgeons to rehearse complex procedures (e.g., liver resection, congenital heart surgery) — reducing operative time and complications.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls — Read Carefully:**
>
> 1. **Confusing SLM and SLS:** Students often interchange Selective Laser **Melting** (SLM, for metals) with Selective Laser **Sintering** (SLS, for polymers). The examiner *will* deduct 1 mark for this in 14-mark answers.
>
> 2. **Wrong unit for VED:** The Volumetric Energy Density unit is **J/mm³**, not J/mm or J/m³. 1 mark lost in derivations for unit errors.
>
> 3. **Forgetting crosslinking in bioprinting:** When describing bioprinting workflows, students often forget to mention the **crosslinking step** (UV, ionic CaCl₂, or thermal). This is a board-pet question — expect at least 1–2 marks for it.
>
> 4. **Confusing "biocompatible" with "biodegradable":** These are *not* the same. Biocompatible = non-toxic in body. Biodegradable = resorbs over time. PCL is both, whereas pure Ti is biocompatible but *not* biodegradable.
>
> 5. **Gibson–Ashby constants:** Do not state $C = 1$ — that is wrong. The correct range is **$C \approx 0.1$–$0.4$** depending on foam topology.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Module-4 Rapid-Revision Bullet Sheet — Biomedical 3D Printing**

- [x] 3D printing = **layer-by-layer additive manufacturing** from a digital CAD model.
- [x] **Five core biomedical metrics:** biocompatibility, porosity (50–80 %), pore size (200–500 μm), strength (2–12 MPa for trabecular bone), and surface roughness.
- [x] **Workflow:** CT/MRI → DICOM → Segmentation → STL → Slicing → G-code → Printing → Post-processing → Sterilization → Implantation.
- [x] **Technologies:** SLA, FDM, SLS, SLM/DMLS, EBM, PolyJet, Bioprinting.
- [x] **Materials:** Polymers (PCL, PLA, PLGA), Metals (Ti-6Al-4V, CoCr, SS 316L), Ceramics (HA, β-TCP), Hydrogels (alginate, gelatin, collagen), Bioinks.
- [x] **Gibson–Ashby equation:** $\sigma^{*} = C \cdot \sigma_{s} \cdot (\rho^{*}/\rho_{s})^{n}$ — links porosity to strength.
- [x] **VED equation:** $E = P/(v \cdot h \cdot t)$ in J/mm³; optimal window for Ti-6Al-4V is 60–120 J/mm³.
- [x] **Hagen–Poiseuille flow:** $\tau_{\text{wall}} = 4\mu Q/(\pi r^{3})$; keep below 5 kPa for cell viability.
- [x] **Bioprinting types:** Inkjet (low viscosity, high resolution), Extrusion (high viscosity, large constructs), Laser-assisted (highest resolution, slow).
- [x] **Clinical applications:** orthopedic implants, dental guides/crowns, cranial plates, surgical models, bioprinted skin and cartilage, drug delivery tablets, prosthetics.
- [x] **ASTM F2792 / ISO/ASTM 52900** is the standard AM classification.
- [x] **Major CAD tools:** Mimics, 3D Slicer, InVesalius, nTopology, Meshmixer.
- [x] **Sterilization methods:** EtO, gamma irradiation, autoclave (only for thermoplastics).
- [x] **Key advantage:** Patient-specific, complex internal lattices, mass customization, low material waste.

**One-line takeaway for the board exam:**
> *"3D printing transforms the patient's own medical imaging into a customized, layer-by-layer fabricated implant or tissue construct — enabling personalized medicine with geometries that traditional manufacturing cannot achieve."*
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Maxillofacial Surgery and Upper Limb Prosthetics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** OEEBT724 - ARTIFICIAL ORGANS & IMPLANTS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Maxillofacial Surgery and Upper Limb Prosthetics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Maxillofacial Surgery — Formal Definition

**Maxillofacial Surgery** is the surgical specialty dedicated to the diagnosis, treatment, and reconstruction of defects, diseases, and injuries of the **head, neck, face, jaws (maxilla and mandible), and the hard and soft tissues of the oral and maxillofacial region.** Within the domain of artificial organs and implants, maxillofacial surgery is intrinsically linked with **craniofacial implantology**, where biocompatible prostheses are used to restore both **form (aesthetics)** and **function (mastication, speech, vision, breathing).**

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight — Module 4**
> The 2024 Scheme specifically emphasizes the *biomaterial selection*, *implant design*, *fixation biomechanics*, and *post-operative rehabilitation strategies* for both **maxillofacial implants** and **upper limb prosthetic devices.**

### Key Anatomical Regions Addressed

| Region | Common Defects | Restoration Goal |
|---|---|---|
| **Mandible (Lower Jaw)** | Tumor resection, trauma, osteoradionecrosis | Restore mastication, facial contour |
| **Maxilla (Upper Jaw)** | Cleft palate, oncology defects | Separate oral and nasal cavities |
| **Orbit** | Enucleation, trauma | House ocular prosthesis |
| **Nasal Complex** | Rhinectomy, congenital absence | Aesthetic + airway patency |
| **Auricle (Ear)** | Microtia, trauma | Cosmesis + hearing aid retention |
| **Cranial Vault** | Craniotomy, trauma | Calvarial protection |

---

## 1.2 Upper Limb Prosthetics — Formal Definition

**Upper Limb Prosthetics** is the branch of rehabilitation engineering concerned with the design, fabrication, fitting, and control of **artificial devices that replace a missing portion of the arm, hand, or fingers** — extending from the finger level up to the shoulder disarticulation. A prosthesis is classified as the entire artificial limb system, comprising a **socket** (interface with the residual limb), **suspension system**, **control mechanism**, and **terminal device**.

> [!NOTE]
> **Foundational Concept:** A *prosthesis* replaces a **missing body part**, whereas an *orthosis* supports or augments an **existing** body part. KTU examiners frequently test this distinction.

---

## 1.3 Intuitive Analogies (Plain-English Explanations)

### Analogy 1: Maxillofacial Implant = "Architectural Scaffolding for the Face"

Imagine your face as a building. When a tumor or accident destroys a load-bearing wall (e.g., the mandible), the structure collapses. A **maxillofacial implant** is like a **pre-fabricated steel beam** that surgeons bolt into the remaining healthy structure (using screws, plates, or osseointegrated fixtures) to **rebuild the architecture** and allow soft tissues (skin, mucosa) to drape over it naturally. Just as architects choose steel vs. concrete based on load, biocompatibility, and corrosion resistance, surgeons select **titanium, PEEK, or PMMA** based on mechanical load, MRI compatibility, and cost.

### Analogy 2: Upper Limb Prosthesis = "Custom Tool Worn Like a Glove"

Think of a missing hand like a missing tool from a toolbox. A **prosthesis** is a **custom-manufactured replacement tool** that mounts onto the "stub" (residual limb) the way a socket wrench mounts onto a bolt. Depending on whether the user wants to *display* the tool (cosmetic), *operate* it via shoulder shrug cables (body-powered), or *drive* it with muscle electrical signals (myoelectric), the engineering complexity — and cost — rises dramatically.

---

## 1.4 Standard Engineering Metrics (Highlighted)

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Performance Metrics for Maxillofacial & Upper-Limb Devices:**
> - **Biocompatibility (ISO 10993):** Mandatory for all implant materials.
> - **Young's Modulus of Bone ≈ 15–20 GPa (cortical)** and **0.5–1 GPa (cancellous)** — implant materials must approximate this to avoid **stress shielding**.
> - **Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V) Modulus ≈ 110 GPa**, **PEEK ≈ 3–4 GPa**, **Co-Cr-Mo ≈ 210 GPa**.
> - **Osseointegration Index:** Direct structural contact (≥ 75%) between implant and living bone at light microscopy level (Albrektsson criteria).
> - **Grip Force Range:** Body-powered hook ≈ 50–100 N; Myoelectric hand ≈ 10–70 N; Passive cosmetic hand ≈ 0 N.
> - **Mass Constraint:** Upper-limb prostheses ideally < 1.5 kg (transradial) and < 2.5 kg (transhumeral) to avoid shoulder fatigue.
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Maxillofacial Surgery — Engineering Analysis

### 2.1.1 Classification of Maxillofacial Defects

Defects are classified by **etiology** and **anatomical extent**:

**By Etiology:**
- **Congenital:** Cleft lip/palate, hemifacial microsomia, Treacher Collins syndrome, craniosynostosis
- **Traumatic:** Road traffic accidents (RTAs), ballistic injuries, burns
- **Oncological:** Ablative tumor surgery (e.g., mandibulectomy, maxillectomy)
- **Infective/Inflammatory:** Osteomyelitis, osteoradionecrosis

**By Anatomical Site (using AO Foundation / Cordeiro classification):**
- Mandibular (central, lateral, hemi)
- Maxillary (low, medium, total maxillectomy)
- Orbital (Type I–VI)
- Composite (through-and-through)

### 2.1.2 Reconstruction Ladder (Hierarchy of Complexity)

The **reconstructive ladder** ranks surgical options from simplest to most complex:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Reconstructive Ladder (Bottom-up = increasing complexity):**
> 1. **Primary closure** (suture directly)
> 2. **Secondary intention** (heal by granulation)
> 3. **Delayed primary closure**
> 4. **Skin graft** (split-thickness / full-thickness)
> 5. **Local flap** (advancement, rotation)
> 6. **Regional flap** (pectoralis major, deltopectoral)
> 7. **Free flap** (fibula, scapula, iliac crest — *gold standard* for mandibular reconstruction)
> 8. **Alloplastic implant** (titanium plate, PEEK patient-specific implant — PSI)

> **Why this matters for KTU:** Questions on "compare autologous vs. alloplastic reconstruction" expect students to cite donor-site morbidity (free flap) vs. infection risk and lack of biological integration (alloplast).

### 2.1.3 Biomaterials Used in Maxillofacial Implants

| Material | Modulus (GPa) | Key Advantage | Key Limitation | Typical Application |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Titanium (cp-Ti, Ti-6Al-4V)** | 110 | Excellent osseointegration, MRI-safe (with caveat) | Stress shielding due to high modulus | Bone plates, screws, dental implants |
| **Co-Cr-Mo Alloy** | 210 | High fatigue strength | Ni allergy, MRI artifact | Load-bearing mandibular plates |
| **316L Stainless Steel** | 200 | Cheap | Corrosion, Ni release | Temporary fixation |
| **PEEK (Polyetheretherketone)** | 3–4 | Modulus close to bone, radiolucent, MRI-safe | Poor osseointegration (needs coating) | Patient-specific cranial/orbital PSIs |
| **PMMA (Bone cement)** | 3 | Moldable, cheap | Exothermic setting, no bone bonding | Cranioplasty, contour filling |
| **Hydroxyapatite (HA)** | 80–110 | Bioactive, osteoconductive | Brittle, low fracture toughness | Coating on metallic implants |
| **Silicone Elastomer** | 0.001–0.05 | Soft, skin-like feel | Capsule formation, migration | External nasal/auricular prosthesis |
| **Porous Polyethylene (Medpor)** | ~1 | Tissue ingrowth, stable | Rigid, not resorbable | Orbital floor reconstruction |

### 2.1.4 Mandibular Reconstruction Biomechanics

The mandible is subjected to **bite forces** during mastication:
- Normal adult bite force: **200–700 N** (molars)
- Post-surgical weakened mandible: as low as **50 N**

The **screw-plate-bone construct** must resist:
- **Bending moments** during chewing
- **Torsional loads** during lateral jaw movement
- **Cyclic fatigue** (≈ 1500 chewing cycles/day)

**Bending stress in a reconstruction plate (4-hole or 2.4 mm miniplate):**

$$\sigma_{bend} = \frac{M \cdot c}{I}$$

Where:
- $M$ = bending moment (N·mm)
- $c$ = half-thickness of plate
- $I$ = second moment of area of cross-section

> **Real-world utility:** Engineers use this equation to decide plate thickness; KTU board questions often ask students to justify *why a 2.4 mm miniplate suffices in the symphysis region but a 2.7 mm reconstruction plate is required for the mandibular body.*

### 2.1.5 Osseointegration (Branemark Concept)

**Definition:** Direct structural and functional connection between ordered, living bone and the surface of a load-bearing artificial implant (Branemark, 1977).

**Criteria for successful osseointegration (Albrektsson):**
1. Implant material biocompatible
2. Design (macro and micro)
3. Surface finish (roughness $R_a > 1 \mu m$ favors bone apposition)
4. Surgical technique (low-trauma, cooling during drilling)
5. Loading conditions (gradual, progressive)
6. Host bone quality

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Pitfall Alert:** Students often confuse *osseointegration* with *biointegration*. Biointegration includes soft-tissue integration (used for percutaneous implants like bone-anchored hearing aids — BAHA).

### 2.1.6 Patient-Specific Implants (PSI) — 3D-Printed Workflow

The modern workflow:

$$CT/MRI \rightarrow DICOM \rightarrow Segmentation \rightarrow CAD \rightarrow 3D Printing (SLM/EBM) \rightarrow Sterilization \rightarrow Surgery$$

> **Why PSI matters:** Patient-specific implants manufactured via **Selective Laser Melting (SLM)** of Ti-6Al-4V powder can replicate exact anatomical curvature, reducing operative time by 25–40% and improving cosmetic outcomes.

---

## 2.2 Upper Limb Prosthetics — Engineering Analysis

### 2.2.1 Levels of Upper Limb Amputation

The prosthesis is named after the **level of amputation:**

| Level | Amputation Site | Prosthesis Name |
|---|---|---|
| **Finger** | Distal/middle/proximal phalanx | Partial hand / finger prosthesis |
| **Transmetacarpal** | Through metacarpals | Partial hand |
| **Wrist disarticulation** | At wrist joint | Wrist disarticulation prosthesis |
| **Transradial (below-elbow, BE)** | Anywhere in forearm | Transradial prosthesis |
| **Elbow disarticulation** | At elbow | Elbow disarticulation prosthesis |
| **Transhumeral (above-elbow, AE)** | Anywhere in humerus | Transhumeral prosthesis |
| **Shoulder disarticulation** | At shoulder joint | Shoulder disarticulation prosthesis |
| **Forequarter** | Includes scapula | Forequarter prosthesis |

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Exam Tip:** A *transradial* prosthesis is much simpler to design than a *transhumeral* one because the user retains the elbow joint. Always note the **residual limb length** as a percentage of the contralateral side — this determines the lever arm and socket design.

### 2.2.2 Classification by Control Mechanism

**A. Passive (Cosmetic) Prostheses**
- Made of silicone, PVC, or foam
- Restore appearance only
- No functional grasp
- Used for partial hand, finger, or when functional goals are minimal

**B. Body-Powered (Conventional) Prostheses**
- Operated by **cables** linked to a **harness** worn on the contralateral shoulder
- Excursion of shoulder → tension on cable → opens/closes terminal device
- Typical cable efficiency: **60–80%**
- Forces transmitted: grip force up to **100 N** (for a hook)

**C. Externally Powered (Myoelectric) Prostheses**
- Use **surface EMG electrodes** on residual muscles
- Signals drive DC motors in hand/wrist/elbow
- Battery-powered (typically Li-ion, 7.4 V, 2000 mAh)
- Multigrip hands (e.g., i-Limb, bebionic) offer 5–24 grip patterns

**D. Hybrid Prostheses**
- Combine body-power and external power
- Typical use: transhumeral amputation with electric elbow + body-powered terminal device
- Solves the "two-function problem" of single-site control

**E. Activity-Specific Prostheses**
- Designed for sports, music, or work (e.g., terminal devices for cycling, holding a camera)

### 2.2.3 Components of an Upper Limb Prosthesis

**Standard anatomy of a transradial prosthesis (proximal → distal):**

1. **Socket** — custom-fitted interface; transmits forces, suspends prosthesis
2. **Suspension system** — pin lock, suction, lanyard, or anatomical (e.g., Muenster socket)
3. **Forearm / Wrist unit** — provides length and connects to terminal device
4. **Terminal device** — hand, hook, or specialized tool
5. **Control system** — cable (body) or electrodes (myoelectric)
6. **Harness** (for body-powered) — figure-8 or chest-strap pattern

### 2.2.4 Myoelectric Control — Signal Processing

**Surface EMG (sEMG) signal flow:**

$$sEMG \rightarrow Pre-amp (Gain \approx 1000) \rightarrow Bandpass Filter (20-500 Hz) \rightarrow Rectifier \rightarrow Envelope Detector \rightarrow Threshold/Classifier \rightarrow Motor Driver$$

**Key parameters for pattern recognition (modern multi-electrode systems):**
- Sampling rate: **1 kHz** minimum
- Window length: **100–200 ms**
- Features: **MAV (Mean Absolute Value), RMS, WL (Waveform Length), ZC (Zero Crossings)**
- Classifier: **LDA, SVM, or CNN (deep learning)**

**Myoelectric decision rule (single-site, two-muscle):**

$$\text{Contract agonist muscle} \rightarrow \text{Hand opens}$$
$$\text{Contract antagonist muscle} \rightarrow \text{Hand closes}$$

### 2.2.5 Socket Design — Pressure Distribution

A well-designed socket distributes **interface pressure** below the capillary occlusion pressure (~**32 mmHg** or **4.3 kPa**) to prevent skin breakdown.

$$P_{interface} = \frac{F_{load}}{A_{contact}}$$

Where:
- $P_{interface}$ = contact pressure
- $F_{load}$ = applied load (body weight or grip reaction)
- $A_{contact}$ = effective contact area

> **Real-world utility:** The Muenster socket for short transradial amputees uses the **biceps tendon** and **medial epicondyle** as suspension anchors, allowing humeral flexion without losing suspension — this is a high-yield KTU question.

### 2.2.6 Osseointegrated Prostheses (Direct Skeletal Attachment)

For amputees unable to tolerate a socket, **osseointegrated (OI) prosthetic limbs** provide a percutaneous bone-anchored attachment.

**Examples:**
- **OPRA (Osseointegrated Prostheses for the Rehabilitation of Amputees)** — Sweden
- **ILP (Integral Limb Prosthesis)** — Germany
- **OGAAP / Phoenix** — UK

**Components:**
1. Endosteal fixture (titanium, threaded, 3–5 mm diameter)
2. Abutment (percutaneous, passes through skin)
3. External prosthetic attachment

**Complications:** infection (superficial 5–15%, deep 1–5%), periprosthetic fracture (2–10%), aseptic loosening.

---

## 2.3 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Formula / Concept | Meaning | Unit / Value |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $\sigma = F/A$ | Stress (tension/compression) | Pa (N/m²) |
| 2 | $\epsilon = \Delta L / L_0$ | Strain | Dimensionless |
| 3 | $E = \sigma / \epsilon$ | Young's Modulus | Pa or GPa |
| 4 | $\sigma_{bend} = M c / I$ | Bending stress in plate | Pa |
| 5 | $I = b h^3 / 12$ | 2nd moment of area (rect.) | m⁴ |
| 6 | $J = \pi d^4 / 32$ | Polar moment (circular shaft) | m⁴ |
| 7 | $\tau_{max} = T r / J$ | Max shear stress in shaft | Pa |
| 8 | $P = F v$ | Power output of prosthesis | W |
| 9 | $\eta = W_{out}/W_{in}$ | Cable/harness efficiency | % (60–80% for body) |
| 10 | $P_{interface} = F/A$ | Socket interface pressure | Pa or N/cm² |
| 11 | $f_{res} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{k/m}$ | Natural frequency of limb | Hz |
| 12 | $SNR_{dB} = 20 \log_{10}(V_{sig}/V_{noise})$ | EMG signal quality | dB |
| 13 | $MAV = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{i=1}^{N} \vert x_i \vert$ | Mean absolute value (EMG) | mV |
| 14 | $V_{out} = G \cdot V_{in}$ | Pre-amplifier gain | V |
| 15 | $L = 10^{(L_{dB}/10)}$ | Loudness perception | dimensionless |

# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivative 1: Bending Stress in a Mandibular Reconstruction Plate

**Problem:** A mandibular reconstruction plate of rectangular cross-section $b = 5$ mm wide, $h = 2$ mm thick experiences a bending moment $M = 0.8$ N·m during mastication. Compute the maximum bending stress.

**Step 1 — Recall the bending stress formula:**

$$\sigma_{bend} = \frac{M \cdot c}{I}$$

**Step 2 — Compute the half-thickness $c$:**

The neutral axis passes through the centroid (geometric center for a uniform rectangle). The maximum stress occurs at the outer fiber, a distance $c$ from the neutral axis. For a rectangle of thickness $h$:

$$c = \frac{h}{2} = \frac{2 \text{ mm}}{2} = 1 \text{ mm} = 1 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}$$

**Step 3 — Compute the second moment of area $I$ for a rectangle:**

$$I = \frac{b \cdot h^3}{12} = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (2 \times 10^{-3})^3}{12}$$

**Step 4 — Expand the cube of $h$:**

$$h^3 = (2 \times 10^{-3})^3 = 8 \times 10^{-9} \text{ m}^3$$

**Step 5 — Substitute into the equation for $I$:**

$$I = \frac{(5 \times 10^{-3}) \cdot (8 \times 10^{-9})}{12} = \frac{40 \times 10^{-12}}{12} = 3.333 \times 10^{-12} \text{ m}^4$$

**Step 6 — Convert $M$ to N·mm for cleaner arithmetic, or keep SI units:**

$$M = 0.8 \text{ N·m} = 800 \text{ N·mm}$$

**Step 7 — Compute the bending stress:**

$$\sigma_{bend} = \frac{800 \text{ N·mm} \cdot 1 \text{ mm}}{3.333 \text{ mm}^4} = 240 \text{ N/mm}^2 = 240 \text{ MPa}$$

**Step 8 — Compare with yield strength of Ti-6Al-4V (≈ 880 MPa):**

$$SF = \frac{\sigma_{yield}}{\sigma_{applied}} = \frac{880}{240} \approx 3.67$$

The factor of safety of 3.67 is **acceptable** for a fatigue-loaded implant, satisfying ISO 5832-3 fatigue criteria for surgical implants.

---

## 3.2 Derivative 2: Socket Interface Pressure Analysis

**Problem:** A transradial prosthetic socket of contact area $A = 80$ cm² is loaded with a body-weight reaction force of $F = 35$ N during a lifting task. Compute the interface pressure and check if it stays below capillary occlusion pressure (≈ 4.3 kPa).

**Step 1 — Convert area to SI units:**

$$A = 80 \text{ cm}^2 = 80 \times 10^{-4} \text{ m}^2 = 8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2$$

**Step 2 — Apply the pressure equation:**

$$P_{interface} = \frac{F}{A} = \frac{35 \text{ N}}{8 \times 10^{-3} \text{ m}^2} = 4375 \text{ Pa} = 4.375 \text{ kPa}$$

**Step 3 — Compare to capillary occlusion pressure:**

$$P_{interface} = 4.375 \text{ kPa} > P_{capillary} = 4.3 \text{ kPa}$$

The pressure **marginally exceeds** the capillary occlusion threshold.

**Step 4 — Engineering conclusion:**

The interface pressure is **borderline tolerable**. The clinician should redesign the socket to either **increase contact area by 5%** (i.e., $A \geq 81.4$ cm²) or **redistribute load** over a wider anatomical region (e.g., humeral epicondyles) to bring pressure below 4.3 kPa and avoid skin breakdown over prolonged use.

---

## 3.3 Derivative 3: Myoelectric Control Threshold Logic

**Problem:** A two-muscle pattern-recognition myoelectric system reads the rectified, smoothed envelope of the *flexor digitorum superficialis* (FDS) and *extensor digitorum communis* (EDC) muscles. Thresholds are set as: Open = 0.3 mV, Close = 0.25 mV. Compute the controller output for the following sequence:

| Time (s) | FDS (mV) | EDC (mV) |
|---|---|---|
| 0.0 | 0.05 | 0.10 |
| 1.0 | 0.05 | 0.45 |
| 2.0 | 0.55 | 0.08 |
| 3.0 | 0.10 | 0.10 |

**Step 1 — Apply decision rule at $t = 0.0$ s:**

FDS = 0.05 (< 0.30), EDC = 0.10 (< 0.25) → **No Action** (idle, motor off)

**Step 2 — Apply decision rule at $t = 1.0$ s:**

FDS = 0.05 (< 0.30) — does not trigger close. EDC = 0.45 (> 0.25) — **Open Hand**

**Step 3 — Apply decision rule at $t = 2.0$ s:**

FDS = 0.55 (> 0.30) — **Close Hand**. EDC = 0.08 (< 0.25) — does not trigger open.

**Step 4 — Apply decision rule at $t = 3.0$ s:**

FDS = 0.10 (< 0.30), EDC = 0.10 (< 0.25) → **No Action** (relaxed, hand holds last position)

**Step 5 — Resulting control sequence:**

$$t = 0: \text{IDLE} \rightarrow t = 1: \text{OPEN} \rightarrow t = 2: \text{CLOSE} \rightarrow t = 3: \text{HOLD}$$

This is the basic **state machine** of a single-site, two-muscle myoelectric controller.

---

## 3.4 Derivative 4: Cable Efficiency in Body-Powered Prosthesis

**Problem:** A body-powered transradial prosthesis has a cable of length $L = 0.45$ m routed over a fairlead. The excursion at the harness is $e_h = 50$ mm, and the resulting excursion at the terminal device is $e_t = 38$ mm. The applied shoulder force is $F_h = 40$ N and the resulting grip force is $F_t = 22$ N. Compute (a) mechanical efficiency of excursion, (b) force efficiency, and (c) overall cable efficiency.

**Step 1 — Excursion efficiency:**

$$\eta_{excursion} = \frac{e_t}{e_h} = \frac{38}{50} = 0.76 = 76\%$$

**Step 2 — Force efficiency:**

$$\eta_{force} = \frac{F_t}{F_h} = \frac{22}{40} = 0.55 = 55\%$$

**Step 3 — Overall efficiency (product of excursion and force efficiency):**

$$\eta_{overall} = \eta_{excursion} \cdot \eta_{force} = 0.76 \cdot 0.55 = 0.418 = 41.8\%$$

**Step 4 — Interpretation:**

A 41.8% overall efficiency is **acceptable** for a transradial body-powered prosthesis. The losses arise from cable friction in the housing, fairlead bending, and elastic stretch. Premium designs (e.g., polyester-coated stainless cables with low-friction PTFE-lined fairleads) can push efficiency toward 60–70%.

---

## 3.5 Derivative 5: Natural Frequency of a Prosthetic Limb

**Problem:** A transhumeral prosthesis has an effective mass $m = 1.8$ kg at the end of a forearm with equivalent stiffness $k = 800$ N/m. Compute the natural frequency of the system to assess resonance against human walking cadence.

**Step 1 — Apply the natural frequency equation:**

$$f_{res} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{k}{m}}$$

**Step 2 — Substitute values:**

$$f_{res} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{\frac{800}{1.8}} = \frac{1}{2\pi}\sqrt{444.44} = \frac{1}{2\pi} \cdot 21.08$$

**Step 3 — Final result:**

$$f_{res} = \frac{21.08}{6.283} \approx 3.36 \text{ Hz}$$

**Step 4 — Compare to walking cadence (≈ 1.5–2.5 Hz):**

The natural frequency (3.36 Hz) is **above** the typical walking cadence, so the prosthesis will not resonate during normal walking. However, during **running (3–4 Hz)**, resonance is possible and may cause discomfort. Engineers typically tune $k$ and $m$ to keep $f_{res} > 4$ Hz.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Decision Matrix — Maxillofacial Implant Material Selection

| Decision Criterion | Titanium | PEEK | PMMA | Co-Cr-Mo | Silicone |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Osseointegration** | Excellent | Poor (needs coating) | None | Good | N/A |
| **Modulus match to bone** | Mismatch (high) | Excellent (close) | Close | Mismatch (high) | Very low |
| **MRI compatibility** | Conditional | Excellent | Conditional | Poor | Excellent |
| **Corrosion resistance** | Excellent | Excellent | Excellent | Good | Excellent |
| **Cost** | High | High | Low | Medium | Medium |
| **Ease of fabrication** | Difficult (machining/SLM) | Moderate (machining/3DP) | Easy (cast in OR) | Difficult | Easy (mold cast) |
| **Load-bearing** | Yes | Limited | Limited | Yes | No |
| **Indications** | Mandible, orbit floor | Cranial PSI, orbit | Cranioplasty | Mandible (legacy) | External nasal/auricular |

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

---

## 5.1 PART A — 3-Mark Questions

### Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Question]
**Define *osseointegration* and list any two factors that influence it.**

**Model Answer:**
Osseointegration, as defined by Branemark, is the **direct structural and functional connection between ordered, living bone and the surface of a load-bearing implant**, with no intervening fibrous tissue.

Two key influencing factors:
1. **Implant surface characteristics** — micro-roughness ($R_a > 1 \mu m$) enhances bone apposition.
2. **Surgical technique** — low-trauma preparation, irrigation to prevent thermal necrosis (drilling temperature must remain below 47 °C for bone viability).

*(Additional factors: biocompatibility, primary stability, loading protocol, host bone quality.)*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Question]
**Differentiate between a *prosthesis* and an *orthosis* with one example each.**

**Model Answer:**

| Feature | Prosthesis | Orthosis |
|---|---|---|
| **Purpose** | Replaces a **missing** body part | Supports or augments an **existing** body part |
| **Example** | Transradial artificial arm | Knee brace for ligament injury |
| **Engineering Goal** | Restore absent function | Restore or protect existing function |
| **Anatomical Relation** | External to body or implanted | Almost always external |

A **prosthesis** substitutes for absent anatomy (e.g., a below-knee artificial leg after amputation), whereas an **orthosis** provides external support to a still-present limb (e.g., an ankle-foot orthosis for foot drop).

---

## 5.2 PART B — 14-Mark Questions (ESE Module Internal Choice Pattern)

### Question A (14 Marks)
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model, 14 Marks, ESE Pattern]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Describe the classification of maxillofacial implants based on anatomical site and explain the materials used in mandibular reconstruction with their advantages and limitations.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** With a neat block diagram, explain the functional components of a myoelectric upper-limb prosthesis. Discuss the role of EMG signal processing in its control.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(a) [7 Marks]

**1. Classification by anatomical site (3 marks):**

Maxillofacial implants are classified by the region they restore:
- **Mandibular implants** — reconstruction plates, condylar prostheses, distraction devices.
- **Maxillary implants** — obturators, zygomatic implants, maxillary sinus lifts.
- **Orbital implants** — orbital floor plates, ocular prostheses (with enucleation implants).
- **Nasal implants** — dorsal L-shaped silicone or PEEK struts.
- **Auricular implants** — bone-anchored hearing aids (BAHA) and cosmetic auricular prostheses.
- **Cranial implants** — cranioplasty meshes and patient-specific PEEK plates.

**2. Materials for mandibular reconstruction (4 marks):**

| Material | Advantages | Limitations |
|---|---|---|
| **Titanium (Ti-6Al-4V)** | High strength-to-weight ratio, excellent osseointegration, MRI-conditional | High modulus (110 GPa) leads to stress shielding |
| **Co-Cr-Mo** | High fatigue strength, legacy workhorse | Nickel allergy risk, MRI artifact |
| **316L Stainless Steel** | Cheap, readily available | Prone to corrosion, allergic to Ni |
| **PEEK** | Modulus (3–4 GPa) close to bone, radiolucent, MRI-safe | Poor osseointegration, requires surface coating |
| **PMMA** | Inexpensive, moldable intra-operatively | Exothermic curing, no osseointegration |
| **Hydroxyapatite (HA)** | Bioactive, osteoconductive | Brittle, low fatigue strength |

**Clinical preference:** For load-bearing mandibular defects, **titanium reconstruction plates with bicortical locking screws** remain the gold standard, often combined with a **vascularized fibula free flap** for biological bone replacement.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-A(b) [7 Marks]

**Functional block diagram of a myoelectric upper-limb prosthesis (4 marks):**

The system flow is:

$$EMG \rightarrow Electrodes \rightarrow Amplifier \rightarrow Filter \rightarrow Rectifier \rightarrow Envelope \rightarrow Classifier \rightarrow Motor Driver \rightarrow Terminal Device$$

- **Electrodes:** Surface Ag/AgCl bipolar electrodes placed on agonist and antagonist muscle bellies (e.g., FDS and EDC for transradial control).
- **Amplifier:** Differential pre-amplifier with CMRR > 80 dB and gain ≈ 1000.
- **Filters:** Bandpass (20–500 Hz) to remove DC drift and high-frequency noise; 50 Hz notch to suppress mains interference.
- **Rectifier & envelope:** Full-wave rectifier followed by a low-pass smoothing filter (typically 4th-order Butterworth, 4 Hz cutoff) to obtain muscle activation envelope.
- **Classifier / Threshold detector:** Single-site two-muscle systems use amplitude thresholds; multi-site systems use LDA, SVM, or CNN for grip-pattern recognition.
- **Motor driver:** H-bridge circuit (PWM-controlled) drives a brushed or brushless DC motor in the terminal device.
- **Terminal device:** Multi-articulating hand, gripper, or specialized tool with onboard sensors (force, position).

**Role of EMG signal processing (3 marks):**

EMG signals carry information about the **user's motor intent.** The signal amplitude (typically 50 µV to 5 mV) is too small to directly drive motors. The signal chain **amplifies, filters, and classifies** these signals to extract intent, which is then mapped to motor commands. **Pattern recognition algorithms** can distinguish 5–10 grip patterns (e.g., power, pinch, key, hook) with classification accuracies of **> 95%** in laboratory settings, enabling intuitive, near-natural prosthetic control.

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Internal Choice Alternative
**[KTU University Exam – Sample Model, 14 Marks, ESE Pattern]**

> **(a) [7 Marks]** Explain the concept of the **Reconstructive Ladder** in maxillofacial surgery. Compare autologous (free flap) reconstruction with alloplastic implant reconstruction.
>
> **(b) [7 Marks]** Describe the **components and working principles** of a body-powered upper-limb prosthesis. Discuss the concept of **cable efficiency** and its practical significance.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(a) [7 Marks]

**1. The Reconstructive Ladder (3 marks):**

The reconstructive ladder is a **hierarchy of surgical options** ordered from simple to complex, used to plan defect closure. Each rung represents an increased level of complexity:

1. Primary closure (direct suturing)
2. Secondary intention (granulation)
3. Skin graft (split-thickness or full-thickness)
4. Local flap
5. Regional flap
6. Free flap (e.g., fibula, scapula)
7. Alloplastic implant (titanium, PEEK)
8. Composite (free flap + implant)

The surgeon climbs the ladder only when simpler options are inadequate. For large mandibular defects after tumor resection, **free fibular flap** (with microvascular anastomosis) is considered the gold standard rung.

**2. Comparison: Autologous vs. Alloplastic (4 marks):**

| Parameter | Autologous (Free Flap) | Alloplastic Implant |
|---|---|---|
| **Source** | Patient's own tissue (fibula, scapula) | Synthetic material |
| **Biological integration** | Excellent (vascularized) | None (osseointegration only at interface) |
| **Donor-site morbidity** | Significant (pain, weakness) | None |
| **Operative time** | 6–12 hours | 2–4 hours |
| **Infection risk** | Moderate | Higher if contaminated (oral cavity) |
| **Customization** | Limited by flap geometry | Patient-specific CAD design possible |
| **Cost** | High (microsurgical skill) | Moderate (implant + OR time) |
| **Longevity** | Permanent biological solution | May require replacement (10–20 yr) |
| **Best for** | Large composite defects, contaminated wounds | Sterile, smaller defects, PSI use |

**Conclusion:** Both modalities are complementary. The choice depends on defect size, location, contamination, patient comorbidities, and surgeon expertise.

---

#### Model Solution for Q-B(b) [7 Marks]

**1. Components of a body-powered prosthesis (4 marks):**

- **Socket:** Custom-molded interface that fits the residual limb (transradial or transhumeral).
- **Suspension system:** Straps, sleeves, or anatomical contours that hold the prosthesis in place.
- **Harness:** Worn over the contralateral shoulder in a figure-8 pattern; the proximal end anchors to the prosthesis, the distal end holds the cable.
- **Control cable:** Stainless-steel cable inside a housing; routed from harness to terminal device.
- **Forearm / shank:** Provides stump-to-terminal-device length compensation; can be endoskeletal (aluminum/carbon-fiber pylon with foam cover) or exoskeletal (rigid shell).
- **Wrist unit:** Allows pronation/supination and quick interchange of terminal devices.
- **Terminal device:** Functional hook, hand, or specialized tool.

**2. Working principle (1 mark):**

Biscapular abduction (shoulder movement) tensions the cable via the harness, transmitting force distally to **open the terminal device** against an internal spring. Releasing the tension allows the spring to **close the device** for grasping.

**3. Cable efficiency and its significance (2 marks):**

Cable efficiency, defined as the **ratio of useful output work to input work**, captures friction losses in the housing, fairlead bending, and cable elasticity. Typical body-powered systems achieve:

- **Force efficiency:** 50–70%
- **Excursion efficiency:** 70–90%
- **Overall efficiency:** 35–60%

**Significance:** Higher efficiency means less shoulder effort is required to operate the prosthesis, reducing user fatigue, secondary musculoskeletal injuries, and rejection of the device. KTU examiners expect students to cite these ranges and the formulas $\eta = W_{out}/W_{in}$ or $\eta = (F_t \cdot e_t)/(F_h \cdot e_h)$.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning / Pitfall Callout

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Deduction Traps in Module 4 Questions:**
> 1. **Mixing up "maxillofacial" with "craniofacial"** — *Maxillofacial* refers to the jaws and face; *craniofacial* extends to the cranium and skull base. Examiners deduct 1 mark if these are interchanged.
> 2. **Confusing osseointegration with biointegration** — Osseointegration is bone-to-implant; biointegration may include soft-tissue integration (e.g., percutaneous abutments).
> 3. **Forgetting the level of amputation in upper-limb questions** — Always specify **transradial vs. transhumeral** when describing socket and control design; omitting this loses 1–2 marks.
> 4. **Writing "silicone implant" for osseous reconstruction** — Silicone is used for **external** prostheses (nose, ear) and is **contraindicated** for load-bearing bone repair.
> 5. **Incorrect conversion of bite force units** — Use Newtons (N) for force, not kgf. Conversion: 1 kgf ≈ 9.81 N.
> 6. **Citing "titanium" generically** — Specify **commercially pure Ti (cp-Ti, Grade 4)** for dental/oral use and **Ti-6Al-4V (Grade 5)** for load-bearing plates.
> 7. **Skipping the "limitations" column in material-comparison tables** — KTU marking scheme requires balanced discussion (advantages **and** limitations) for full marks.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid-Revision Checklist)

- [x] **Maxillofacial surgery** restores defects of the jaws, face, and cranium caused by trauma, oncology, or congenital anomalies.
- [x] The **reconstructive ladder** orders options from primary closure (simple) to composite flaps with implants (most complex).
- [x] **Free fibula flap** is the gold standard for large mandibular reconstruction.
- [x] **Osseointegration** (Branemark) = direct bone-to-implant contact without fibrous tissue.
- [x] **Albrektsson's six criteria:** material, design, surface, surgical technique, loading, host bone.
- [x] **Stress shielding** occurs when implant modulus (Ti ≈ 110 GPa) is much higher than bone (≈ 15–20 GPa cortical) — addressed by PEEK (≈ 3–4 GPa).
- [x] **Patient-specific implants (PSI)** follow the CT → DICOM → CAD → 3D-print workflow.
- [x] **Upper-limb amputation levels** include finger, transmetacarpal, transradial, elbow disarticulation, transhumeral, shoulder disarticulation, forequarter.
- [x] **Prosthesis types:** cosmetic (passive), body-powered (cable), myoelectric (EMG), hybrid, activity-specific.
- [x] **Body-powered efficiency** typically 35–60%; powered by contralateral shoulder harness.
- [x] **Myoelectric control** uses surface EMG (50 µV – 5 mV), threshold or pattern recognition, drives DC motors.
- [x] **EMG signal chain:** electrode → differential amp (CMRR > 80 dB) → bandpass (20–500 Hz) → notch (50 Hz) → rectifier → envelope → classifier → motor driver.
- [x] **Socket interface pressure** should remain below **capillary occlusion pressure (~4.3 kPa)** to prevent skin breakdown.
- [x] **Osseointegrated percutaneous prostheses** (OPRA, ILP) are alternatives for socket-intolerant amputees but carry infection risk (5–15% superficial).
- [x] **Capillary occlusion threshold:** ~32 mmHg ≈ 4.3 kPa.
- [x] **Prosthesis ≠ Orthosis** — replacement vs. support.
- [x] **Natural frequency of a prosthetic limb** should be > 4 Hz to avoid resonance during running.
- [x] **3D printing techniques for implants:** Selective Laser Melting (SLM), Electron Beam Melting (EBM), Fused Deposition Modeling (FDM, for PEEK/plastics).
- [x] **Always mention both advantages and limitations** when discussing any biomaterial.
- [x] **Key cross-link to biomechanics:** bending stress $\sigma = Mc/I$, socket pressure $P = F/A$, cable efficiency $\eta = W_{out}/W_{in}$.
- [x] **Common KTU-exam topics from this module:** compare prosthesis vs. orthosis; reconstructive ladder; osseointegration; biomaterials for maxillofacial implants; myoelectric vs. body-powered; osseointegrated percutaneous prostheses.
